Author: SalsabeelKhaleel
Title: How UX Is Changing in the Age of AI
Text:
How UX Is Changing in the Age of AI
As AI becomes a normal part of everyday software through features like Automation and Generation, our usual UX patterns are no longer enough. The role of UX has to change to continue serving human needs in systems where the software can act on its own.
1. The Need for Visibility
In traditional software, the user starts the action, so they naturally understand what is happening.
BUT …
In agentic design, In agent-like systems, the software can take actions in the background.
This means users need clear visibility into what happened and what is happening right now is the Human entry point rather than call to actions.
1. The Need to Set Constraints
The UX must include simple configuration interfaces for setting rules and compliance constraints that restricts and limit the agent’s probabilistic behavior.
The UX Must include a description on processes an agent needs to follow , like a timeline or a state machine to support automation.
Design should allow:
- Simple rule-setting and limits
- Clear steps and processes the system must follow
- A defined path that guides the system’s behavior
2. The Need for Monitoring
The UX must provide constant, clear, real-time indicators of what the agent is doing, what its next step is, and what environment it is interacting with
Like with Action Narration (Audit Logs), Every autonomous action must be logged and narrated, providing a detailed history of what the agent did.
Layered Explainability: Users need to understand why the agent made a decision.
Design should include:
- Logs of actions the system performed
- Short explanations of why those actions were taken
- Information on what the system plans to do next
2. The need for Control
Since agents are inherently probabilistic and can take unexpected paths, the UX must prioritize human control.
Intervention Points: The UX must explicitly design for Human in the Loop controls, allowing users to review / interfere /confirm high stake or irreversible actions before execution.
Simple Overrides: Easily accessible mechanisms (like prominent Pause, Stop, or Cancel buttons) must be available for users to intervene.
Recovery Mechanisms: The design must anticipate agent missteps, providing clear error messaging and options for the user to re-direct the agent.
Design should support:
- Clear moments where the user can review or approve important steps
- Easy buttons to pause, stop, or cancel actions
- Helpful ways to correct or redirect the system when it makes a mistake
